title: Beech bread bin
subtitle: A dovetail-jointed beech bread bin with a fitted drop-on lid
excerpt: A dovetail-jointed beech bread bin 400 mm wide, 220 mm deep, and 240 mm tall, with a fitted drop-on lid. The four corners are half-blind dovetails; the base sits in a stopped groove; the lid has a small shoulder that drops into the opening. This is an advanced joinery project for a maker confident in dovetail layout and chopping.
sourceNotes: Victorian cabinet-making manuals for box and bin construction; Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for dovetail joinery methods.

content.text: Keep both hands behind the cutting edge at all times. Secure the workpiece in a vice before chopping dovetails; do not hold it free in the hand.
Beech is the correct wood for a bread bin: hard, closed-grained, and odour-neutral. The
at each corner keeps the end-grain of the tail board from showing on the front face of the bin; the joint is strong and the front face is clean. The base sits in a
so that the groove does not show on the bottom edge of the front or back walls.

Seasoned beech, planed on all faces. Two side walls: 220 by 240 by 18 mm. Front and back walls: 400 by 240 by 18 mm. Base: 400 by 220 by 9 mm. Lid panel: 402 by 222 by 12 mm, with a 3 mm shoulder around the edge.

Set the sliding bevel to 1:8 for beech (1 unit rise to 8 units across). Mark five tails on each side wall. The half-blind lap must be at least 6 mm; any thinner and the saw or chisel will break through the front face. Mark the tails with a marking knife; saw the cheeks and chop the waste in stages, working from both faces.

Transfer the tail positions directly onto the front and back wall pin boards by holding the tail board in position and marking with a knife. Chop the waste from the pin sockets working from the inside face. Dry-fit all four corners before running any glue; a bread bin is large enough that a glue-up under time pressure is stressful.

Cut the stopped grooves for the base before final assembly: 9 mm wide, 6 mm deep, stopping 18 mm from the front edge on each wall. The base slides in from the back before the back wall is glued. The lid is a flat panel with a 3 mm shoulder around the perimeter; plane the shoulder to fit the opening, testing often. The lid should drop into the opening under its own weight with a slight piston resistance, not a thud.

Smooth all surfaces with a smoothing plane and card scraper. Beech does not require an oil finish; it can be left unfinished or treated with a light coat of beeswax. Oil or lacquer finishes on the inside of a bread bin will taint bread; leave the interior bare.

items.fix: Test the lid in the opening and mark the tight spots with a pencil rubbed on the shoulder edge. Plane only the marked areas; re-test after every two strokes. Fit work requires very small adjustments.
items.cause: Shoulder not planed consistently all round; one or two sides are slightly thinner than the others
items.symptom: Lid drops too far in or lifts unevenly
